Roman Catholic society of apostolic life

Paris Foreign Missions Society

|  |  |

The **Society of Foreign Missions of Paris** ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Société des Missions Etrangères de Paris*, pronounced [[sɔsjete de misjɔ̃z etʁɑ̃ʒɛʁ də paʁi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French"), **MEP**) is a [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") [missionary organization](/wiki/Missionary_order "Missionary order"). It is not a [religious institute](/wiki/Religious_institute "Religious institute"), but an organization of [secular priests](/wiki/Secular_clergy "Secular clergy") and [lay persons](/wiki/Laity "Laity") dedicated to missionary work in foreign lands.[[4]](#cite_note-Asia-4)

The Society of Foreign Missions of Paris was established 1658–63. In 1659, instructions for establishment of the Paris Foreign Missions Society were given by Rome's [Sacred Congregation for the Propagation of the Faith](/wiki/Sacred_Congregation_for_the_Propagation_of_the_Faith "Sacred Congregation for the Propagation of the Faith"). This marked the creation of a missionary institution that, for the first time, did not depend on the control of the traditional missionary and [colonial powers](/wiki/Colonialism "Colonialism") of Spain or Portugal.[[5]](#cite_note-5) In the 350 years since its foundation, the institution has sent more than 4,200 missionary priests to Asia and North America. Their mission is to adapt to local customs and languages, develop a native [clergy](/wiki/Clergy "Clergy"), and keep close contacts with Rome.[[6]](#cite_note-Missions-6)

In the 19th century, local persecutions of missionary priests of the Paris Foreign Missions Society were often a pretext for French military intervention in Asia.[[7]](#cite_note-Missions_2-7) In [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"), such persecutions were used by the French government to justify the armed interventions of [Jean-Baptiste Cécille](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste_C%C3%A9cille "Jean-Baptiste Cécille") and [Charles Rigault de Genouilly](/wiki/Charles_Rigault_de_Genouilly "Charles Rigault de Genouilly"). In China, the murder of the priest [Auguste Chapdelaine](/wiki/Auguste_Chapdelaine "Auguste Chapdelaine") became the *[casus belli](/wiki/Casus_belli "Casus belli")* for the French involvement in the [Second Opium War](/wiki/Second_Opium_War "Second Opium War") in 1856. In [Korea](/wiki/Korea "Korea"), persecutions were used to justify the 1866 [French campaign against Korea](/wiki/French_Campaign_against_Korea,_1866 "French Campaign against Korea, 1866").

Today, the Paris Foreign Missions Society remains an active institution in the evangelization of Asia.

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paris_Foreign_Missions_Society&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/MEP_in_Paris.jpg/250px-MEP_in_Paris.jpg)](/wiki/File:MEP_in_Paris.jpg)

Building of the Missions étrangères de Paris, 128 [Rue du Bac](/wiki/Rue_du_Bac "Rue du Bac"), Paris.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/MEP_symbol.jpg/250px-MEP_symbol.jpg)](/wiki/File:MEP_symbol.jpg)

Symbol of the Missions Étrangères de Paris.

The traditional colonial powers of Spain and Portugal had initially received from the [Pope](/wiki/Pope "Pope") an exclusive agreement to evangelize conquered lands, a system known as *[Padroado Real](/wiki/Padroado_Real "Padroado Real")* in Portuguese and *[Patronato real](/wiki/Patronato_real "Patronato real")* in Spanish. After some time however, Rome grew dissatisfied with the *Padroado* system, due to its limited means, strong involvement with politics, and dependence on the kings of Spain and Portugal for any decision.[[8]](#cite_note-Mantienne-8)

From a territorial standpoint also, Portugal had been losing ground against the new colonial powers of England and the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic"), meaning that it was becoming less capable of evangelizing new territories.[[9]](#cite_note-Les_Missions_Etrangères,_p.30-9) In territories that it used to control, Portugal had seen some disasters; for instance, [Japanese Christianity](/wiki/Kirishitan "Kirishitan") was eradicated from around 1620.[[10]](#cite_note-Les_Missions-10)

Finally, Catholic officials had doubts about the efficacy of religious orders, such as the [Dominicans](/wiki/Dominican_Order "Dominican Order"), [Franciscans](/wiki/Franciscans "Franciscans"), [Jesuits](/wiki/Jesuits "Jesuits") or [Barnabites](/wiki/Barnabites "Barnabites"), since they were highly vulnerable in case of persecutions. They did not seem able to develop local clergy, who would be less vulnerable to state persecution. Sending bishops to develop a strong local clergy seemed to be the solution to achieve future expansion:[[10]](#cite_note-Les_Missions-10)

> "We have all reason to fear that what happened to the Church of Japan could also happen to the Church of Annam, because these kings, in [Tonkin](/wiki/Tonkin "Tonkin") as well as in [Cochinchina](/wiki/Cochinchina "Cochinchina"), are very powerful and accustomed to war... It is necessary that the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See"), by its own movement, give pastors to these Oriental regions where Christians multiply in a marvellous way, lest, without bishops, these men die without sacrament and manifestly risk damnation." [Alexandre de Rhodes](/wiki/Alexandre_de_Rhodes "Alexandre de Rhodes").[[10]](#cite_note-Les_Missions-10)

As early as 1622, [Pope Gregory XV](/wiki/Pope_Gregory_XV "Pope Gregory XV"), wishing to take back control of the missionary efforts, had established the [Sacred Congregation for the Propagation of the Faith](/wiki/Sacred_Congregation_for_the_Propagation_of_the_Faith "Sacred Congregation for the Propagation of the Faith") (*Congregatio de Propaganda Fide*, commonly known as *[Propaganda](/wiki/Congregation_for_the_Evangelization_of_Peoples "Congregation for the Evangelization of Peoples")*) with the objective of bringing non-Catholic Christians to the Catholic faith, and also inhabitants of the American continent and Asia.[[8]](#cite_note-Mantienne-8) In order to do so, Rome resurrected the system of [Apostolic vicars](/wiki/Apostolic_vicariate "Apostolic vicariate"), who would report directly to Rome in their missionary efforts, and would be responsible to create a native clergy.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In the field, violent conflicts would erupt between the *[Padroado](/wiki/Padroado "Padroado")* and the *Propaganda* during the 17th and 18th centuries.[[8]](#cite_note-Mantienne-8) (When the first missionaries of the Paris Foreign Missions Society were sent to the Far East, the Portuguese missionaries were ordered to capture them and send them to [Lisbon](/wiki/Lisbon "Lisbon")).[[12]](#cite_note-12) The creation of the Paris Foreign Missions Society was well-aligned with Rome's efforts to develop the role of the *Propaganda*.[[13]](#cite_note-Mantienne_2-13)

## Establishment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paris_Foreign_Missions_Society&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Establishment")]

See also: [French school of spirituality](/wiki/French_school_of_spirituality "French school of spirituality")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Derhodes.jpg/250px-Derhodes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Derhodes.jpg)

The French Jesuit [Alexandre de Rhodes](/wiki/Alexandre_de_Rhodes "Alexandre de Rhodes") is at the origin of the creation of the Paris Foreign Missions Society.

The creation of the Paris Foreign Missions Society was initiated when the Jesuit priest [Alexandre de Rhodes](/wiki/Alexandre_de_Rhodes "Alexandre de Rhodes"), back from Vietnam and asking for the dispatch of numerous missionaries to the [Far East](/wiki/Far_East "Far East"), obtained in 1650 an agreement by [Pope Innocent X](/wiki/Pope_Innocent_X "Pope Innocent X") to send [secular priests](/wiki/Secular_clergy "Secular clergy") and bishops as missionaries.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Alexandre de Rhodes received in Paris in 1653 a strong financial and organizational support from the [Compagnie du Saint-Sacrement](/wiki/Compagnie_du_Saint-Sacrement "Compagnie du Saint-Sacrement") for the establishment of the Paris Foreign Missions Society.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) Alexander de Rhodes found [secular clergy](/wiki/Secular_clergy "Secular clergy") volunteers in Paris in the persons of [François Pallu](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Pallu "François Pallu") and [Pierre Lambert de la Motte](/wiki/Pierre_Lambert_de_la_Motte "Pierre Lambert de la Motte") and later [Ignace Cotolendi](/wiki/Ignace_Cotolendi "Ignace Cotolendi"), the first members of the Paris Foreign Missions Society, who were sent to the Far-East as [Apostolic vicariate](/wiki/Apostolic_vicariate "Apostolic vicariate").[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Due to the strong opposition of Portugal and the death of Pope Innocent X the project was stalled for several years however, until the candidates to the missions decided to go by themselves to Rome in June 1657.[[9]](#cite_note-Les_Missions_Etrangères,_p.30-9)

### Appointment of missionary bishops

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paris_Foreign_Missions_Society&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Appointment of missionary bishops")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Mgr_Francois_Pallu.jpg/250px-Mgr_Francois_Pallu.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mgr_Francois_Pallu.jpg)

[François Pallu](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Pallu "François Pallu"), founding father of the Paris Foreign Missions Society

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Mgr_Lambert_de_la_Motte.jpg/250px-Mgr_Lambert_de_la_Motte.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mgr_Lambert_de_la_Motte.jpg)

[Pierre Lambert de la Motte](/wiki/Pierre_Lambert_de_la_Motte "Pierre Lambert de la Motte") (1624–1679)

On 29 July 1658, the two chief founders of the Paris Foreign Missions Society were appointed as bishops in the [Vatican](/wiki/St._Peter%27s_Basilica "St. Peter's Basilica"), becoming Pallu, Bishop of [Heliopolis in Augustamnica](/wiki/Heliopolis_(ancient_Egypt) "Heliopolis (ancient Egypt)"), [Vicar Apostolic](/wiki/Vicar_Apostolic "Vicar Apostolic") of [Tonkin](/wiki/Tonkin "Tonkin"), and Lambert de la Motte, Bishop of [Berytus](/wiki/Berytus "Berytus"), Vicar Apostolic of [Cochinchina](/wiki/Cochinchina "Cochinchina").[[20]](#cite_note-Les_Missions_2-20) On 9 September 1659, the papal bull *[Super cathedram principis apostolorum](http://www.papalencyclicals.net/Alex07/alex07super.htm)* by [Pope Alexander VII](/wiki/Pope_Alexander_VII "Pope Alexander VII") defined the territories they would have to administer: for Pallu, Tonkin, [Laos](/wiki/Laos "Laos"), and five adjacent provinces of southern China ([Yunnan](/wiki/Yunnan "Yunnan"), [Guizhou](/wiki/Guizhou "Guizhou"), [Huguang](/wiki/Huguang_Province "Huguang Province"), [Sichuan](/wiki/Sichuan "Sichuan"), [Guangxi](/wiki/Guangxi "Guangxi")), for Lambert de la Motte, Cochinchina and five provinces of southeastern China ([Zhejiang](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang"), [Fujian](/wiki/Fujian "Fujian"), [Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong"), [Jiangxi](/wiki/Jiangxi "Jiangxi"), [Hainan](/wiki/Hainan "Hainan")).[[20]](#cite_note-Les_Missions_2-20) In 1660 the third founder was appointed as Cotolendi, Bishop of [Metellopolis](/wiki/Motella "Motella"), Vicar Apostolic of [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing "Nanjing"), with also five provinces of China,[[20]](#cite_note-Les_Missions_2-20) namely [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing"), [Shanxi](/wiki/Shanxi "Shanxi"), [Shandong](/wiki/Shandong "Shandong"), [Korea](/wiki/Korea "Korea") and [Tartary](/wiki/Tartary "Tartary").

All of them were nominated bishops *[in partibus infidelium](/wiki/In_partibus_infidelium "In partibus infidelium")* ("In areas of the [Infidels](/wiki/Infidels "Infidels")", i.e., [Heliopolis](/wiki/Baalbek "Baalbek"), [Beirut](/wiki/Beirut "Beirut"), [Metellopolis](/wiki/Metellopolis "Metellopolis") etc...), receiving long-disappeared bishopric titles from areas that had been lost, in order not to compromise contemporary bishopric titles and avoid conflicts with the bishoprics established through the *padroado* system.[[13]](#cite_note-Mantienne_2-13) In 1658 also, [François de Laval](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_de_Laval "François de Laval") was nominated Vicar Apostolic of Canada,[[21]](#cite_note-21) and [Bishop of Petra](/wiki/Petra "Petra") *in partibus infidelium*, becoming the first Bishop of [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France"), and in 1663 he would found the [Séminaire de Québec](/wiki/S%C3%A9minaire_de_Qu%C3%A9bec "Séminaire de Québec") with the support of the Paris Foreign Missions Society.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

The society itself ("Assemblée des Missions") was formally established by the Compagnie du Saint-Sacrement in 1658.[[23]](#cite_note-Mantienne_3-23) The object of the new society was and is still the evangelization of non-Christian countries, by founding churches and raising up a native clergy under the jurisdiction of the bishops. The Society was officially recognized in 1664.[[24]](#cite_note-24) The creation of the Paris Foreign Missions Society coincided with the establishment of the [French East India Company](/wiki/Louis_XIV%27s_East_India_Company "Louis XIV's East India Company").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Mgr_Ignace_Cotolendi.jpg/250px-Mgr_Ignace_Cotolendi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mgr_Ignace_Cotolendi.jpg)

[Ignace Cotolendi](/wiki/Ignace_Cotolendi "Ignace Cotolendi") (1630–1662)

In order to dispatch the three missionaries to Asia, the Compagnie du Saint-Sacrement established a trading company, the [Compagnie de Chine](/wiki/Compagnie_de_Chine_(1660-1664) "Compagnie de Chine (1660-1664)"), founded in 1660. A ship, the *Saint-Louis*, was built in the Netherlands by the shipowner Fermanel, but the ship foundered soon after being launched.[[25]](#cite_note-25) At the same time, the establishment of a trading company and the perceived threat of French missionary efforts to Asia was met with huge opposition by the [Jesuits](/wiki/Jesuits "Jesuits"), the [Portuguese](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal"), the [Dutch](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") and even the *Propaganda*, leading to the issuing of an interdiction of the Compagnie du Saint-Sacrement by [Cardinal Mazarin](/wiki/Cardinal_Mazarin "Cardinal Mazarin") in 1660.[[26]](#cite_note-Asia_2-26) In spite of these events, the King, the Assembly of the French Clergy, the Compagnie du Saint-Sacrement and private donors accepted to finance the effort, and the three bishop managed to depart, although they now had to travel on land.[[26]](#cite_note-Asia_2-26)

The three bishops chosen for Asia left France (1660–62) to go to their respective missions, and crossed [Persia](/wiki/Persia "Persia") and India on foot, since Portugal would have refused to take non-*Padroado* missionaries by ship, and the Dutch and the English refused to take Catholic missionaries.[[6]](#cite_note-Missions-6) Lambert left [Marseille](/wiki/Marseille "Marseille") on 26 November 1660, and reached [Mergui](/wiki/Mergui "Mergui") in Siam 18 months later, Pallu joined Lambert in the capital of [Siam](/wiki/Siam "Siam") [Ayutthaya](/wiki/Ayutthaya_Kingdom "Ayutthaya Kingdom") after 24 months overland, and Cotolendi died upon arrival in India on 6 August 1662.[[6]](#cite_note-Missions-6) Siam thus became the first country to receive the evangelization efforts of the Paris Foreign Missions Society, to be followed by new missions years later in Cochinchina, Tonkin and parts of China.[[6]](#cite_note-Missions-6)

### Founding principles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paris_Foreign_Missions_Society&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Founding principles")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/MEP_Chapell.jpg/250px-MEP_Chapell.jpg)](/wiki/File:MEP_Chapell.jpg)

Chapel of the Paris Foreign Missions Society

The mission had the objective of adapting to local customs, establishing a native [clergy](/wiki/Clergy "Clergy"), and keeping close contacts with Rome.[[6]](#cite_note-Missions-6) In 1659, instructions were given by the [Sacred Congregation for the Propagation of the Faith](/wiki/Sacred_Congregation_for_the_Propagation_of_the_Faith "Sacred Congregation for the Propagation of the Faith") (known as the "Propaganda"):

> "Here is the principal reason which determined the Sacred Congregation to send you as Bishops in these regions. It is that you endeavour, by all possible means and methods, to educate young people so as to make them capable of receiving [priesthood](/wiki/Priesthood "Priesthood")."

— Extract from the 1659 Instructions, given to Pallu and Lambert de la Motte by the Sacred Congregation for the Propagation of the Faith.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

Instructions were also given to the effect that respecting the habits of the countries to be evangelized was paramount, a guiding principle of the missions ever since:

> "Do not act with [zeal](/wiki/Zealotry "Zealotry"), do not put forward any arguments to convince these peoples to change their [rites](/wiki/Ritual "Ritual"), their customs or their usages, except if they are evidently contrary to the religion and [morality](/wiki/Morality "Morality"). What would be more absurd than to bring France, Spain, Italy or any other European country to the Chinese? Do not bring to them our countries, but instead bring to them the faith, a faith that does not reject or hurt the rites, nor the usages of any people, provided that these are not distasteful, but that instead keeps and protects them."

— Extract from the 1659 Instructions, given to Pallu and Lambert de la Motte by the Sacred Congregation for the Propagation of the Faith.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

### Establishment on Rue du Bac, Paris

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paris_Foreign_Missions_Society&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Establishment on Rue du Bac, Paris")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/MEP_in_1663.jpg/250px-MEP_in_1663.jpg)](/wiki/File:MEP_in_1663.jpg)

The Paris Foreign Missions Society in 1663

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Turgot_1739_map_MEP_detail.jpg/250px-Turgot_1739_map_MEP_detail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Turgot_1739_map_MEP_detail.jpg)

The Paris Foreign Missions Society in 1739, with its park (detail of 1739 map of Paris by [Turgot](/wiki/Michel-%C3%89tienne_Turgot "Michel-Étienne Turgot")).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/MEP_Chapell_exteriors.jpg/250px-MEP_Chapell_exteriors.jpg)](/wiki/File:MEP_Chapell_exteriors.jpg)

The Chapel at the Paris Foreign Missions Society, established in 1691.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Commemorative_medal_of_the_first_stone_of_the_Mission_1683.jpg/250px-Commemorative_medal_of_the_first_stone_of_the_Mission_1683.jpg)](/wiki/File:Commemorative_medal_of_the_first_stone_of_the_Mission_1683.jpg)

[Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV_of_France "Louis XIV of France") commemorative medal of the first stone of the Mission, 1683

The seminary (*Séminaire des Missions Étrangères*) was created in March 1663. [Jean Duval](/wiki/Jean_Duval "Jean Duval"), ordained under the name Bernard de Sainte Thérèse and nominated Bishop of Babylon (modern [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq")) in 1638, offered the deserted buildings of his own Seminary for Missions to Persia, which he had created in 1644 at 128 [Rue du Bac](/wiki/Rue_du_Bac "Rue du Bac").[[23]](#cite_note-Mantienne_3-23) On 10 March 1664, [Vincent de Meur](/w/index.php?title=Vincent_de_Meur&action=edit&redlink=1 "Vincent de Meur (page does not exist)") was nominated as the first director of the seminary, and officially became superior of the seminary on 11 June 1664.

The seminary was established so that the society might recruit members and administer its property, through the actions of the [Compagnie du Saint-Sacrement](/wiki/Compagnie_du_Saint-Sacrement "Compagnie du Saint-Sacrement") and by the priests whom the vicars Apostolic had appointed their agents. This house, whose directors were to form young priests to the apostolic life and transmit to the bishops the offerings made by charity, was, and still is situated in Paris in the Rue du Bac.

Known from the beginning as the [Seminary](/wiki/Seminary "Seminary") of Foreign Missions, it secured the approval of [Pope Alexander VII](/wiki/Pope_Alexander_VII "Pope Alexander VII"), and the legal recognition of the French government and [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV") in 1663. In 1691 the chapel was established, and in 1732 the new, larger, building was completed.[[23]](#cite_note-Mantienne_3-23)

Another wing, perpendicular to the 1732 one, was added in the 19th century to accommodate the great increase in members of the seminary.

## 1658–1800

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paris_Foreign_Missions_Society&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1658–1800")]

The chief events of this period were: the publication of the book *Institutions apostoliques*, which contains the germ of the principles of the rule, the foundation of the general seminary in [Ayutthaya](/wiki/Ayutthaya_(city) "Ayutthaya (city)"), [Siam](/wiki/Siam "Siam")[[29]](#cite_note-29) (the *[Seminary of Saint Joseph](/wiki/Seminary_of_Saint_Joseph "Seminary of Saint Joseph")*,[[30]](#cite_note-30) at the origin of the [College General](/wiki/College_General "College General") now in [Penang](/wiki/Penang "Penang"), Malaysia), the evangelization of Tonkin, Cochinchina, Cambodia, and Siam, where more than 40,000 Christians were baptized, the creation of an institute of [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_people "Vietnamese people") nuns known as "[Lovers of the Cross](/wiki/Lovers_of_the_Cross "Lovers of the Cross")", the establishment of rules among catechists, and the ordination of thirty native priests. Between 1660 and 1700 about 100 missionaries were sent to Asia.[[4]](#cite_note-Asia-4)

### Siam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paris_Foreign_Missions_Society&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Siam")]

See also: [France-Thailand relations](/wiki/France-Thailand_relations "France-Thailand relations")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/SiameseEmbassyToLouisXIV1686NicolasLarmessin.jpg/250px-SiameseEmbassyToLouisXIV1686NicolasLarmessin.jpg)](/wiki/File:SiameseEmbassyToLouisXIV1686NicolasLarmessin.jpg)

The Siamese embassy to [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV"), led by [Kosa Pan](/wiki/Kosa_Pan "Kosa Pan") in 1686, was a result of the missionary efforts of the Paris Foreign Missions Society.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Audience_with_Narai%2C_1685-10-18_%28b%29.jpg/250px-Audience_with_Narai%2C_1685-10-18_%28b%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Audience_with_Narai,_1685-10-18_(b).jpg)

[Louis Laneau](/wiki/Louis_Laneau "Louis Laneau") of the Paris Foreign Missions Society (on the right, forefront)[[31]](#cite_note-Les_Missions_Etrangeres,_p.54-31) was closely involved in the contacts with the Siamese king [Narai](/wiki/Narai "Narai"). Here, the French ambassador [Chevalier de Chaumont](/wiki/Chevalier_de_Chaumont "Chevalier de Chaumont") presents a letter from [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV") to King Narai in 1685.

The Paris Foreign Missions Society started its work in [Siam](/wiki/Siam "Siam"), with the establishment of a base in its capital [Ayutthaya](/wiki/Ayutthaya_Kingdom "Ayutthaya Kingdom"). Siam was known to be highly tolerant of other religions and was the only country in Southeast Asia where the Catholic fathers could establish a base safely.[[32]](#cite_note-32) With the agreement of the Siamese king [Narai](/wiki/Narai "Narai"), the [Seminary of Saint Joseph](/wiki/Seminary_of_Saint_Joseph "Seminary of Saint Joseph") was established, which could educate Asian candidate priests from all the countries of the Southeast Asian peninsula. A cathedral was also constructed. The college remained in Siam for a century, until the conquest of Siam by [Burma](/wiki/Burma "Burma") in 1766.[[31]](#cite_note-Les_Missions_Etrangeres,_p.54-31)

The missionary work also had political effects: through their initiative a more active trade was established among Indo-China, the [Indies](/wiki/Indies "Indies"), and France; embassies were sent from place to place; and treaties were signed. In 1681, [Jacques-Charles de Brisacier](/wiki/Jacques-Charles_de_Brisacier "Jacques-Charles de Brisacier") was elected superior of the organization. In 1681 or 1682, the Siamese king [Narai](/wiki/Narai "Narai"), who was seeking to reduce Dutch and English influence, named the French medical missionary Brother [René Charbonneau](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Charbonneau "René Charbonneau"), a member of the Siam mission, as Governor of [Phuket](/wiki/Phuket_Province "Phuket Province") . Charbonneau held the position of Governor until 1685.[[33]](#cite_note-33) In 1687 a [French expedition to Siam](/wiki/Franco-Thai_relations#Embassy_of_Loubère-Céberet_(1687) "Franco-Thai relations") took possession of [Bangkok](/wiki/Bangkok "Bangkok"), [Mergui](/wiki/Mergui "Mergui"), and [Jonselang](/wiki/Jonselang "Jonselang"). France came close to ruling an Indo-Chinese empire, though it failed following the 1688 [Siamese revolution](/wiki/Siamese_revolution_(1688) "Siamese revolution (1688)"), which adversely affected the missions. [Louis Laneau](/wiki/Louis_Laneau "Louis Laneau") of the society was involved in these events.[[6]](#cite_note-Missions-6) He was imprisoned by the government for two years, with half of the members of the Seminar, until he was allowed to resume his activities.

In 1702, [Artus de Lionne](/wiki/Artus_de_Lionne "Artus de Lionne"), Bishop of Rosalie, and missionary of the Paris Foreign Missions Society, brought [Arcadio Huang](/wiki/Arcadio_Huang "Arcadio Huang") to France as one of the first Chinese men there. He established the basis for the study of the Chinese language in France.

In the second half of the 18th century, the society was charged with the missions which the Jesuits had possessed in India prior to suppression of the order in Portugal. Many of the Jesuits remained in Asia. The missions took on a new life, especially in [Sichuan](/wiki/Sichuan "Sichuan") (see [Catholic Church in Sichuan](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Sichuan "Catholic Church in Sichuan")), under bishops [Pottier](/w/index.php?title=Fran%C3%A7ois_Pottier&action=edit&redlink=1 "François Pottier (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Pottier "fr:François Pottier")] and [Dufresse](/wiki/Gabriel-Taurin_Dufresse "Gabriel-Taurin Dufresse"), and in [Cochinchina](/wiki/Cochinchina "Cochinchina").

### Cochinchina

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paris_Foreign_Missions_Society&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Cochinchina")]

See also: [France-Vietnam relations](/wiki/France-Vietnam_relations "France-Vietnam relations") and [French assistance to Nguyễn Ánh](/wiki/French_assistance_to_Nguy%E1%BB%85n_%C3%81nh "French assistance to Nguyễn Ánh")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Pigneau_de_Behaine_portrait.jpg/250px-Pigneau_de_Behaine_portrait.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pigneau_de_Behaine_portrait.jpg)

[Pigneau de Behaine](/wiki/Pigneau_de_Behaine "Pigneau de Behaine") acted as a diplomatic agent for the Vietnamese Prince [Nguyễn Phúc Ánh](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_Ph%C3%BAc_%C3%81nh "Nguyễn Phúc Ánh") (the future [Gia Long](/wiki/Gia_Long "Gia Long")).

In Cochinchina, [Pigneau de Behaine](/wiki/Pigneau_de_Behaine "Pigneau de Behaine") acted as an agent for [Nguyễn Phúc Ánh](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_Ph%C3%BAc_%C3%81nh "Nguyễn Phúc Ánh"), a pretender to the throne, in making a treaty with France (the 1787 [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles_(1787) "Treaty of Versailles (1787)")). Pigneau de Behaine assisted Nguyễn Phúc Ánh in obtaining the support of several French soldiers and officers, modernizing his army, and ultimately gaining victory over the [Tây Sơn](/wiki/T%C3%A2y_S%C6%A1n "Tây Sơn").

### French revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paris_Foreign_Missions_Society&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: French revolution")]

At the end of the 18th century, the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") halted the growth of the society, which had previously been very rapid. At that time it had six bishops, a score of missionaries, assisted by 135 native priests; in the various missions there were nine seminaries with 250 students, and 300,000 Christians.[[7]](#cite_note-Missions_2-7) Each year the number of baptisms rose on an average of 3000 to 3500; that of infant baptisms *in articulo mortis* was more than 100,000.

## 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paris_Foreign_Missions_Society&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 19th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Chapelle_de_l%27%C3%89piphanie_-_Le_D%C3%A9part.jpg/250px-Chapelle_de_l%27%C3%89piphanie_-_Le_D%C3%A9part.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chapelle_de_l%27%C3%89piphanie_-_Le_D%C3%A9part.jpg)

Departure ceremony at the Paris Foreign Missions Society. *Le Départ*, 1868, by [Charles Louis de Fredy de Coubertin](/wiki/Charles_Louis_de_Fredy_de_Coubertin "Charles Louis de Fredy de Coubertin").

On 23 March 1805, [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") signed a decree reinstating the Paris Foreign Missions Society.[[34]](#cite_note-34) In 1809 however, following a conflict with the Pope, Napoleon cancelled his decision. The Missions would be firmly re-established through a decree by [Louis XVIII](/wiki/Louis_XVIII "Louis XVIII") in March 1815.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

Several causes contributed to the rapid growth of the society in the 19th century; chiefly the charity of the Propagation of the Faith and the [Society of the Holy Childhood](/wiki/Society_of_the_Holy_Childhood "Society of the Holy Childhood"). Each bishop received annually 1200 [francs](/wiki/Francs "Francs"), each mission had its general needs and works allowance, which varied according to its importance, and could amount to from 10,000 to 30,000 francs.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/MEP_Departures_of_1856.jpg/250px-MEP_Departures_of_1856.jpg)](/wiki/File:MEP_Departures_of_1856.jpg)

1856 departures of MEP missionaries

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/1864_MEP_departures.jpg/250px-1864_MEP_departures.jpg)](/wiki/File:1864_MEP_departures.jpg)

1864 departures of MEP missionaries. The four on the left would become martyrs in Korea.

The second cause was persecution. Fifteen missionaries died in prison or were beheaded during the 17th and 18th centuries and the beginning of the 19th century; but after that those killed among the missionaries were very numerous. (See [Martyr Saints of China](/wiki/Martyr_Saints_of_China "Martyr Saints of China")). Altogether, about 200 MEP missionaries died of violent death. Among them 23 were beatified, of whom 20 were canonized,[[36]](#cite_note-36) with an additional 3 in 2000.

Authors such as [Chateaubriand](/wiki/Chateaubriand "Chateaubriand"), with his *[Génie du christianisme](/wiki/G%C3%A9nie_du_christianisme "Génie du christianisme")*, also contributed to the recovery of the militant spirit of Catholicism, after the troubles of the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution").[[37]](#cite_note-37)

By 1820, the territory of the Missions, which included India since the prohition of the [Company of Jesus](/wiki/Company_of_Jesus "Company of Jesus") (the [Jesuits](/wiki/Jesuits "Jesuits")) in 1776, extended to Korea, Japan, [Manchuria](/wiki/Manchuria "Manchuria"), [Tibet](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Tibet "Catholic Church in Tibet"), [Burma](/wiki/Burma "Burma"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia") etc...[[7]](#cite_note-Missions_2-7)

In the 19th century, the local persecutions of missionary priests of the Paris Foreign Missions Society was often a pretext for French military intervention in Asia,[[7]](#cite_note-Missions_2-7) based on the doctrine of the [Protectorate of missions](/wiki/Protectorate_of_missions "Protectorate of missions"). These persecutions were described in Europe by books, pamphlets, annals, and journals, inspiring numerous young men either with the desire for [martyrdom](/wiki/Martyrdom "Martyrdom") or that of evangelization. They played a part in inspiring European nations, especially France and England, to intervene in [Indochina](/wiki/Indochina "Indochina") and China.[[7]](#cite_note-Missions_2-7) However, in late nineteenth century Indochina, missionaries from the Foreign Missions Society often were at odds the staunchly secular French state, which questioned their commitment to the colonial cause.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

Another cause of the progress of the missionaries was the ease and frequency of communication in consequence of the invention of steam and the opening of the [Suez Canal](/wiki/Suez_Canal "Suez Canal"). A voyage could be made safely in one month which formerly required eight to ten months amid many dangers. In Vietnam, the persecutions of numerous priests such as [Pierre Borie](/wiki/Pierre_Borie "Pierre Borie") or [Augustin Schoeffer](/wiki/Augustin_Schoeffer "Augustin Schoeffer") was used as a justification for the armed interventions of [Jean-Baptiste Cécille](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste_C%C3%A9cille "Jean-Baptiste Cécille") and [Charles Rigault de Genouilly](/wiki/Charles_Rigault_de_Genouilly "Charles Rigault de Genouilly"), ultimately leading to the occupation of Vietnam and the creation of [French Indochina](/wiki/French_Indochina "French Indochina"). In Korea, the beheading of [Siméon-François Berneux](/wiki/Sim%C3%A9on-Fran%C3%A7ois_Berneux "Siméon-François Berneux") and other priests justified the 1866 [French Campaign against Korea](/wiki/French_Campaign_against_Korea,_1866 "French Campaign against Korea, 1866").

### Vietnam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paris_Foreign_Missions_Society&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Vietnam")]

See also: [Martyrs of Vietnam](/wiki/Martyrs_of_Vietnam "Martyrs of Vietnam"), [Catholic Church in Vietnam](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Vietnam "Catholic Church in Vietnam"), and [France-Vietnam relations](/wiki/France-Vietnam_relations "France-Vietnam relations")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Martyrdom_of_Joseph_Marchand.jpg/250px-Martyrdom_of_Joseph_Marchand.jpg)](/wiki/File:Martyrdom_of_Joseph_Marchand.jpg)

Martyrdom of [Joseph Marchand](/wiki/Joseph_Marchand "Joseph Marchand") in Vietnam in 1835

In 1825, emperor [Minh Mạng](/wiki/Minh_M%E1%BA%A1ng "Minh Mạng"), the son and successor of [Gia Long](/wiki/Gia_Long "Gia Long"), prohibited foreign missionaries in Vietnam, on the grounds that they perverted the people. The prohibition proved largely ineffective, as missionaries continued their activities in Vietnam, and participated in armed rebellions against Minh Mạng, as in the [Lê Văn Khôi revolt](/wiki/L%C3%AA_V%C4%83n_Kh%C3%B4i_revolt "Lê Văn Khôi revolt") (1833–1835). He banned Catholicism completely, as well as French and Vietnamese priests (1833–1836), leading to persecutions of French missionaries.[[39]](#cite_note-39) These included the martyrdom of [Joseph Marchand](/wiki/Joseph_Marchand "Joseph Marchand") in 1835 or [Pierre Borie](/wiki/Pierre_Borie "Pierre Borie") in 1838. These events served in France to stoke a desire among young men to intervene and protect the Catholic faith.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Matyrdom_of_Saint_Pierre_Borie_1838_Vietnam.jpg/250px-Matyrdom_of_Saint_Pierre_Borie_1838_Vietnam.jpg)](/wiki/File:Matyrdom_of_Saint_Pierre_Borie_1838_Vietnam.jpg)

[Martyrdom](/wiki/Martyrdom "Martyrdom") of [Pierre Borie](/wiki/Pierre_Borie "Pierre Borie"), 24 November 1838, in Tonkin, Vietnam; Vietnamese painting.

Ming Man's successor, [Thiệu Trị](/wiki/Thi%E1%BB%87u_Tr%E1%BB%8B "Thiệu Trị"), upheld the anti-Catholic policy of his predecessor. In 1843, the French Foreign Minister [François Guizot](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Guizot "François Guizot") sent a fleet to Vietnam under Admiral [Jean-Baptiste Cécille](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste_C%C3%A9cille "Jean-Baptiste Cécille") and Captain Charner,.[[40]](#cite_note-Google_page_5-40) The action also was related to the British successes in China in 1842, and France hoped to be able to establish trade with China from the south. The pretext was to support British efforts in China, and to fight the persecution of French missionaries in Vietnam.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

In 1847, Cécille sent two warships (*Gloire* and *Victorieuse*) under Captain Lapierre to [Da Nang](/wiki/Da_Nang "Da Nang") (Tourane) in Vietnam to obtain the release of two imprisoned French missionaries, Bishop [Dominique Lefèbvre](/wiki/Dominique_Lef%C3%A8bvre "Dominique Lefèbvre") (imprisoned for a second time as he had re-entered Vietnam illegally) and Duclos, and freedom of worship for Catholics in Vietnam.[[40]](#cite_note-Google_page_5-40)[[42]](#cite_note-Tucker-42) As negotiations drew on without results, on 15 April 1847, a fight named the [Bombardment of Đà Nẵng](/wiki/Bombardment_of_Tourane_(1847) "Bombardment of Tourane (1847)") erupted between the French fleet and Vietnamese ships, three of which were sunk as a result. The French fleet sailed away.[[42]](#cite_note-Tucker-42)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Jean-Charles_Cornay.jpg/250px-Jean-Charles_Cornay.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jean-Charles_Cornay.jpg)

Martyrdom of [Jean-Charles Cornay](/wiki/Jean-Charles_Cornay "Jean-Charles Cornay"), 1837

Other missionaries were martyred during the reign of Emperor [Tự Đức](/wiki/T%E1%BB%B1_%C4%90%E1%BB%A9c "Tự Đức"), such as [Augustin Schoeffer](/wiki/Augustin_Schoeffer "Augustin Schoeffer") in 1851 and [Jean Louis Bonnard](/wiki/Jean_Louis_Bonnard "Jean Louis Bonnard") in 1852, prompting the Paris Foreign Missions Society to ask the French government for a diplomatic intervention.[[43]](#cite_note-43) In 1858, [Charles Rigault de Genouilly](/wiki/Charles_Rigault_de_Genouilly "Charles Rigault de Genouilly") attacked Vietnam under the orders of [Napoleon III](/wiki/Napoleon_III "Napoleon III") following the failed mission of diplomat [Charles de Montigny](/wiki/Charles_de_Montigny "Charles de Montigny"). His stated mission was to stop the persecution of Catholic missionaries in the country and assure the unimpeded propagation of the faith.[[44]](#cite_note-44) Rigault de Genouilly, with 14 French gunships, 3,000 men and 300 Filipino troops provided by the Spanish,[[45]](#cite_note-45) attacked the port of Da Nang in 1858, causing significant damage, and occupying the city. After a few months, Rigault had to leave due to problems with supplies and illnesses among many of his troops.[[46]](#cite_note-46) Sailing south, De Genouilly [captured Saigon](/wiki/Capture_of_Saigon "Capture of Saigon"), a poorly defended city, on 18 February 1859. This was the beginning of the [French conquest of Cochinchina](/wiki/French_conquest_of_Cochinchina "French conquest of Cochinchina").

### Korea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paris_Foreign_Missions_Society&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Korea")]

See also: [Korean Martyrs](/wiki/Korean_Martyrs "Korean Martyrs"); [Catholic Church in Korea](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Korea "Catholic Church in Korea"); and [French Campaign against Korea, 1866](/wiki/French_Campaign_against_Korea,_1866 "French Campaign against Korea, 1866")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/5/59/Saint_Laurent-Marie-Joseph_Imbert%2C_M.E.P._%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Saint_Laurent-Marie-Joseph_Imbert,_M.E.P._(2).jpg)

[Laurent-Joseph-Marius Imbert](/wiki/Laurent-Joseph-Marius_Imbert "Laurent-Joseph-Marius Imbert") of the Paris Foreign Missions Society, Saint, martyred in Korea (21 September 1839).

In the mid-19th century that the first western Catholic missionaries began to enter Korea. This was done by stealth, either via the Korean border with Manchuria or the Yellow Sea. These French missionaries of the Paris Foreign Missions Society arrived in Korea in the 1840s to proselytize to a growing Korean flock that had in fact independently introduced Catholicism into Korea but needed ordained ministers.

#### 1839 persecutions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paris_Foreign_Missions_Society&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: 1839 persecutions")]

On 26 April 1836, [Laurent-Joseph-Marius Imbert](/wiki/Laurent-Joseph-Marius_Imbert "Laurent-Joseph-Marius Imbert") of the Paris Foreign Missions Society was appointed [Vicar Apostolic](/wiki/Vicar_Apostolic "Vicar Apostolic") of Korea and [Titular Bishop](/wiki/Titular_bishop "Titular bishop") of [Capsa](/wiki/Capsa_(see) "Capsa (see)"). On 14 May 1837, he was [ordained](/wiki/Ordination "Ordination") Titular Bishop of Capsa and crossed secretly from Manchuria to Korea the same year. On 10 August 1839, Bishop Imbert, who was secretly going about his missionary work, was betrayed. He was taken to [Seoul](/wiki/Seoul "Seoul") where he was tortured to reveal the whereabouts of foreign missionaries. He wrote a note to his fellow missionaries, [Pierre-Philibert Maubant](/w/index.php?title=Pierre-Philibert_Maubant&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pierre-Philibert Maubant (page does not exist)") and [Jacques-Honoré Chastan](/w/index.php?title=Jacques-Honor%C3%A9_Chastan&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jacques-Honoré Chastan (page does not exist)"), asking them to surrender to the Korean authorities as well. They were taken before an interrogator and questioned for three days to reveal the names and whereabouts of their converts. As torture failed to break them down, they were sent to another prison and beheaded on 21 September 1839 at [Saenamteo](/wiki/Saenamteo "Saenamteo"). Their bodies remained exposed for several days but were finally buried on Noku Mountain.

#### 1866 persecutions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paris_Foreign_Missions_Society&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: 1866 persecutions")]

Bishop [Siméon-François Berneux](/wiki/Sim%C3%A9on-Fran%C3%A7ois_Berneux "Siméon-François Berneux"), appointed in 1856 as head of the infant Korean Catholic church, estimated in 1859 that the number of Korean faithful had reached nearly 17,000. At first the Korean court turned a blind eye to such incursions. This attitude changed abruptly, however, with the enthronement of [King Gojong](/wiki/King_Gojong_of_Joseon "King Gojong of Joseon") in 1864. By the time the Heungseon Daewongun assumed de facto control of the government in 1864 there were twelve French Paris Foreign Missions Society priests living and preaching in Korea and an estimated 23,000 native Korean converts.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/St-Berneux.jpg/250px-St-Berneux.jpg)](/wiki/File:St-Berneux.jpg)

[Bishop Berneux](/wiki/Sim%C3%A9on-Fran%C3%A7ois_Berneux "Siméon-François Berneux") of the Paris Foreign Missions Society was tortured and then beheaded on 7 March 1866.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

In January 1866 Russian ships appeared on the east coast of Korea demanding trading and residency rights in what seemed an echo of the demands made on China by other western powers. Native Korean Christians, with connections at court, saw in this an opportunity to advance their cause and suggested an alliance between France and Korea to repel the Russian advances, suggesting further that this alliance could be negotiated through Bishop Berneux. The Heungseon Daewongun seemed open to this idea, though it is uncertain whether this was ruse to bring the head of the Korean Catholic Church out into the open. Berneux was summoned to the capital, but upon his arrival in February 1866, he was seized and executed. A roundup then began of the other French Catholic priests and native converts.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Pierre_Henri_Dorie_Korea_1864.jpg/250px-Pierre_Henri_Dorie_Korea_1864.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pierre_Henri_Dorie_Korea_1864.jpg)

[Pierre Henri Dorié](/wiki/Pierre_Henri_Dori%C3%A9 "Pierre Henri Dorié") of the Paris Foreign Missions Society, also martyred in Korea in 1866.

As a result of the Korean dragnet all but three of the French missionaries were captured and executed: among them were Siméon Berneux, Antoine Daveluy, Just de Bretenières, [Louis Beaulieu](/wiki/Louis_Beaulieu "Louis Beaulieu"), [Pierre Henri Dorié](/wiki/Pierre_Henri_Dori%C3%A9 "Pierre Henri Dorié"), Pierre Aumaître, Luc Martin Huin, all of them members of the Paris Foreign Missions Society and canonized by [Pope John Paul II](/wiki/Pope_John_Paul_II "Pope John Paul II") on 6 May 1984. An untold number of Korean Catholics also met their end (estimations run around 10,000),[[48]](#cite_note-48) many being executed a place called [Jeoldu-san](/wiki/Jeoldu-san "Jeoldu-san") in Seoul on the banks of the Han River. In late June 1866 one of the three surviving French missionaries, Felix-Claire Ridel, managed to escape via a fishing vessel and make his way to [Tianjin](/wiki/Tianjin "Tianjin"), China in early July 1866. Fortuitously in Tianjin at the time of Ridel's arrival was the commander of the French Far Eastern Squadron, Rear Admiral [Pierre-Gustave Roze](/wiki/Pierre-Gustave_Roze "Pierre-Gustave Roze"). Hearing of the massacre and the affront to French national honor, Roze determined to launch a [punitive expedition](/wiki/Punitive_expedition "Punitive expedition"), the [French Campaign against Korea, 1866](/wiki/French_Campaign_against_Korea,_1866 "French Campaign against Korea, 1866").

### Japan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paris_Foreign_Missions_Society&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Japan")]

See also: [France-Japan relations (19th century)](/wiki/France-Japan_relations_(19th_century) "France-Japan relations (19th century)") and [Catholic Church in Japan](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Japan "Catholic Church in Japan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/MEP_Fathers_and_Seminarists_in_Southern_Japan_1881.jpg/250px-MEP_Fathers_and_Seminarists_in_Southern_Japan_1881.jpg)](/wiki/File:MEP_Fathers_and_Seminarists_in_Southern_Japan_1881.jpg)

MEP Fathers and seminarists in southern Japan in 1881

After the [suppression of Christianity in Japan](/wiki/Kirishitan "Kirishitan") from around 1620 and nearly two century of strictly enforced seclusion thereafter, various contacts occurred from the middle of the 19th century as France was trying to expand its influence in Asia. After the signature of the [Treaty of Nanking](/wiki/Treaty_of_Nanking "Treaty of Nanking") by Great Britain in 1842, both France and the United States tried to increase their efforts in the Orient.

The first attempts at resuming contacts occurred with the [Ryūkyū Kingdom](/wiki/Ry%C5%ABky%C5%AB_Kingdom "Ryūkyū Kingdom") (modern [Okinawa](/wiki/Okinawa_Prefecture "Okinawa Prefecture")), a vassal of the Japanese fief of [Satsuma](/wiki/Satsuma_Domain "Satsuma Domain") since 1609. In 1844, a French naval expedition under Captain Fornier-Duplan onboard *Alcmène* visited [Okinawa](/wiki/Okinawa_Prefecture "Okinawa Prefecture") on 28 April 1844. Trade was denied, but Father Forcade of the Paris Foreign Missions Society was left behind with a Chinese translator, named Auguste Ko. Forcade and Ko remained in the Temple of Amiku, city of [Tomari](/wiki/Tomari,_Okinawa "Tomari, Okinawa"), under strict surveillance, only able to learn the Japanese language from monks. After a period of one year, on 1 May 1846, the French ship *Sabine*, commanded by Guérin, arrived, soon followed by *La Victorieuse*, commanded by [Charles Rigault de Genouilly](/wiki/Charles_Rigault_de_Genouilly "Charles Rigault de Genouilly"), and *Cléopâtre*, under [Admiral Cécille](/wiki/Admiral_C%C3%A9cille "Admiral Cécille"). They came with the news that [Pope Gregory XVI](/wiki/Pope_Gregory_XVI "Pope Gregory XVI") had nominated Forcade [Bishop](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop") of [Samos](/wiki/Samos "Samos") and [Vicar Apostolic](/wiki/Vicar_Apostolic "Vicar Apostolic") of Japan.[[52]](#cite_note-52) Cécille offered the kingdom French protection against British expansionism, but in vain, and only obtained that two missionaries could stay.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Mgr_Petitjean_first_Vicar_Apostolic_of_Japan.jpg/250px-Mgr_Petitjean_first_Vicar_Apostolic_of_Japan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mgr_Petitjean_first_Vicar_Apostolic_of_Japan.jpg)

Petitjean, of the Paris Foreign Missions Society, first effective Vicar Apostolic of Japan

Forcade and Ko were picked up to be used as translators in Japan, and Leturdu was left in Tomari, soon joined by Mathieu Adnet. On 24 July 1846, Admiral Cécille arrived in [Nagasaki](/wiki/Nagasaki "Nagasaki"), but failed in his negotiations and was denied landing, and Bishop Forcade never set foot in mainland Japan.[[53]](#cite_note-53) The Ryu-Kyu court in [Naha](/wiki/Naha "Naha") complained in early 1847 about the presence of the French missionaries, who had to be removed in 1848.

France would have no further contacts with Okinawa for the next 7 years, until news came that [Commodore Perry](/wiki/Matthew_C._Perry "Matthew C. Perry") had obtained an agreement with the islands on 11 July 1854, following his treaty with Japan. France sent an embassy under Rear-Admiral Cécille onboard *La Virginie* in order to obtain similar advantages. A convention was signed on 24 November 1855.

As contacts between France and Japan developed during the [Bakumatsu](/wiki/Bakumatsu "Bakumatsu") period (on the military side this is the period of the first [French military mission to Japan](/wiki/French_military_mission_to_Japan_(1867%E2%80%931868) "French military mission to Japan (1867–1868)")), Japan was formed into a unique Vicariate Apostolic from 1866 until 1876. The Vicariate was administered by [Bernard Petitjean](/wiki/Bernard_Petitjean "Bernard Petitjean"), of the Paris Foreign Missions Society (1866–1884).[[2]](#cite_note-Catholic-2)

A [sanatorium](/wiki/Sanatorium "Sanatorium") for sick missionaries was established in Hong Kong ([Béthanie](/wiki/B%C3%A9thanie_(Hong_Kong) "Béthanie (Hong Kong)"));[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[2]](#cite_note-Catholic-2) another in India among the [Nilgiri](/wiki/Nilgiris_(mountains) "Nilgiris (mountains)") mountains, and a third in France. In Hong Kong there were also a house of spiritual retreat and a printing establishment ([Nazareth](/wiki/University_Hall_(University_of_Hong_Kong) "University Hall (University of Hong Kong)")) which published works of art of the Far East – dictionaries, grammars, books of theology, piety, Christian doctrine, and [pedagogy](/wiki/Pedagogy "Pedagogy").[[55]](#cite_note-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[2]](#cite_note-Catholic-2) Houses of correspondence, or agencies, were established in the Far East, in [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"), Hong Kong, [Saigon](/wiki/Saigon "Saigon"), Singapore, and one in [Marseille](/wiki/Marseille "Marseille"), France.[[2]](#cite_note-Catholic-2)

## Exhibits

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paris_Foreign_Missions_Society&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Exhibits")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Salle_des_Martyrs_MEP.jpg/250px-Salle_des_Martyrs_MEP.jpg)](/wiki/File:Salle_des_Martyrs_MEP.jpg)

*Salle des Martyrs*. The ladder-like apparatus in the middle is the [cangue](/wiki/Cangue "Cangue") that was worn by [Pierre Borie](/wiki/Pierre_Borie "Pierre Borie") in captivity.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/The_Virgin_Mary_disguised_as_Kanon_Japan.jpg/250px-The_Virgin_Mary_disguised_as_Kanon_Japan.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Virgin_Mary_disguised_as_Kanon_Japan.jpg)

The [Virgin Mary](/wiki/Virgin_Mary "Virgin Mary") disguised as [Kannon](/wiki/Kannon "Kannon"), [Kirishitan](/wiki/Kirishitan "Kirishitan") cult, 17th century Japan. Salle des Martyrs, Paris Foreign Missions Society.

The [crypt](/wiki/Crypt "Crypt") at the Paris Foreign Missions Society headquarters located Rue du Bac houses a permanent display called "Salle des Martyrs" ("Room of the Martyrs"). Numerous artifacts are on display, mainly remains and relics of martyred members of the missions, depictions of various martyrdoms endured during the history of the missions, and objects related to the [Catholic Faith](/wiki/Catholic_Faith "Catholic Faith") in the various countries of Asia. Also, historical archives and graphic material are available, regarding the details of the missions. The Salle des Martyrs can be visited for free from Tuesday to Saturday, from 11:00 to 18:30, and on Sundays from 13:00 to 18:00.

Another, much larger, exhibition is located on the ground floor of the main building of the Paris Foreign Missions Society. Established as a temporary exhibition in 2007–2008, it remains in place but is now closed to the general public. It is only opened for visits once a year during the free-access "Journée des Musées Nationaux", although there seem to be plans to make it a permanent exhibition in the near future.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Korean_martyrs_stela_MEP.jpg/250px-Korean_martyrs_stela_MEP.jpg)](/wiki/File:Korean_martyrs_stela_MEP.jpg)

Stela to the members of the Paris Foreign Missions Society who were [martyred in Korea](/wiki/Korean_Martyrs "Korean Martyrs").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Rigault_de_Genouilly_Canton_Chinese_bell_at_the_MEP.jpg/250px-Rigault_de_Genouilly_Canton_Chinese_bell_at_the_MEP.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rigault_de_Genouilly_Canton_Chinese_bell_at_the_MEP.jpg)

Chinese bell brought from [Canton](/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou") by Charles Rigault de Genouilly, now in the park of the Paris Foreign Missions Society.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/House_of_Chateaubriand.jpg/250px-House_of_Chateaubriand.jpg)](/wiki/File:House_of_Chateaubriand.jpg)

House of [Chateaubriand](/wiki/Chateaubriand "Chateaubriand"), 120 Rue du Bac, with view on the park of the Paris Foreign Missions Society.

The park of the Paris Foreign Missions Society is the largest private garden in Paris. It houses various significant artifacts, such as a Chinese bell from [Canton](/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou") brought to France by the French Admiral [Charles Rigault de Genouilly](/wiki/Charles_Rigault_de_Genouilly "Charles Rigault de Genouilly"), a stela to [Korean Martyrs](/wiki/Korean_Martyrs "Korean Martyrs") and the list of canonized members of the Paris Foreign Missions Society. The park can be visited every Saturday at 15:30.

The French writer [Chateaubriand](/wiki/Chateaubriand "Chateaubriand") lived in an apartment 120 Rue du Bac, with a view on the park, a fact he mentions in the last paragraph of his *[Mémoires d'Outre-Tombe](/wiki/M%C3%A9moires_d%27Outre-Tombe "Mémoires d'Outre-Tombe")*:

> "As I write these last words, my window, which looks west over the gardens of the Foreign Mission, is open: it is six in the morning; I can see the pale and swollen moon; it is sinking over the spire of the [Invalides](/wiki/Invalides "Invalides"), scarcely touched by the first golden glow from the East; one might say that the old world was ending, and the new beginning. I behold the light of a dawn whose sunrise I shall never see. It only remains for me to sit down at the edge of my grave; then I shall descend boldly, crucifix in hand, into eternity."

— Chateaubriand *Mémoires d'Outre-Tombe* Book XLII: Chapter 18[[57]](#cite_note-57)

As part of the 117 [Martyrs of Vietnam](/wiki/Martyrs_of_Vietnam "Martyrs of Vietnam"), 10 martyrs of the M.E.P. were canonized by [Pope John Paul II](/wiki/Pope_John_Paul_II "Pope John Paul II") on 19 June 1988, including 11 [Dominican](/wiki/Dominican_Order "Dominican Order") priests, 37 Vietnamese priests, and 59 Vietnamese laity:

* [François-Isidore Gagelin](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois-Isidore_Gagelin "François-Isidore Gagelin") (10 May 1799 - 17 October 1833), priest
* [Joseph Marchand](/wiki/Joseph_Marchand "Joseph Marchand") (17 August 1803 - 30 November 1835), priest
* [Jean-Charles Cornay](/wiki/Jean-Charles_Cornay "Jean-Charles Cornay") (27 February 1809 - 20 September 1837), priest
* François Jaccard (16 September 1799 - 21 September 1838), priest
* [Pierre Borie](/wiki/Pierre_Dumoulin-Borie "Pierre Dumoulin-Borie") (20 February 1808 - 24 November 1838), Titular Bishop of Achantus and Vicar Apostolic of Western Tonking
* [Augustin Schoeffler](/wiki/Augustin_Schoeffler "Augustin Schoeffler") (22 November 1822 - 1 May 1851), priest
* [Jean-Louis Bonnard](/wiki/Jean-Louis_Bonnard "Jean-Louis Bonnard") (1 March 1824 - 1 May 1852), priest
* Pierre-François Néron (21 September 1818 - 3 November 1860), priest
* [Théophane Vénard](/wiki/Th%C3%A9ophane_V%C3%A9nard "Théophane Vénard") (21 November 1829 - 2 February 1861), priest
* Étienne-Théodore Cuenot (8 February 1802 - 14 November 1861), Titular Bishop of Metellopolis and Vicar Apostolic of Eastern Cochinchina

As part of the 103 [Martyrs of Korea](/wiki/Korean_Martyrs "Korean Martyrs"), 10 martyrs of the M.E.P. were canonized by [Pope John Paul II](/wiki/Pope_John_Paul_II "Pope John Paul II") on 6 May 1984,including [Andrew Kim Taegon](/wiki/Andrew_Kim_Taegon "Andrew Kim Taegon"), the first Korean priest, and 92 Korean laity:

* [Laurent-Marie-Joseph Imbert](/wiki/Laurent-Joseph-Marius_Imbert "Laurent-Joseph-Marius Imbert") (15 April 1797 - 21 September 1839), Titular Bishop of Capsus and Apostolic Vicar of Korea
* Pierre-Philibert Maubant (20 September 1803 - 21 September 1839), priest
* Jacques-Honore Chastan (7 October 1803 - 21 September 1839), priest
* [Siméon-Francois Berneux](/wiki/Sim%C3%A9on-Fran%C3%A7ois_Berneux "Siméon-François Berneux") (14 May 1814 - 7 March 1866), Titular Bishop of Capsus and Apostolic Vicar of Korea
* Sinon-Marie-Just Ranfer de Bretenières (28 February 1838 - 7 March 1866), priest
* [Bernard-Louis Beaulieu](/wiki/Louis_Beaulieu "Louis Beaulieu") (8 October 1840 - 7 March 1866), priest
* [Pierre-Henri Dorie](/wiki/Pierre-Henri_Dorie "Pierre-Henri Dorie") (23 September 1839 - 7 March 1866), priest
* [Antoine Daveluy](/wiki/Marie-Nicolas-Antoine_Daveluy "Marie-Nicolas-Antoine Daveluy") (16 March 1818 - 30 March 1866), Titular Bishop of Akka and Coadjutor Apostolic Vicar of Korea
* Pierre Aumaître (April 1837 - 30 March 1866), priest
* Martin-Luc Huin (20 October 1836 - 30 March 1866), priest

As part of 120 [Martyrs of China](/wiki/Martyrs_of_China "Martyrs of China"), 3 missionaries of the M.E.P. were canonized by [Pope John Paul II](/wiki/Pope_John_Paul_II "Pope John Paul II") on 1 October 2000, including 9 [Franciscans](/wiki/Franciscans "Franciscans"), 6 [Dominicans](/wiki/Dominican_Order "Dominican Order"), 7 Franciscan missionary sisters of Mary, 1 [Lazarist](/wiki/Lazarist "Lazarist"), 1 Italian priest of the Foreign Missions of Milan, 4 Chinese priests and 83 Chinese laity:

* [Gabriel-Taurin Dufresse](/wiki/Gabriel-Taurin_Dufresse "Gabriel-Taurin Dufresse") (8 December 1750 - 14 September 1815), Titular Bishop of Thrabaca and [Vicar Apostolic of Szechwan](/wiki/Diocese_of_Chengdu "Diocese of Chengdu")
* [Auguste Chapdelaine](/wiki/Auguste_Chapdelaine "Auguste Chapdelaine") (6 January 1814 - 29 February 1856), priest
* [Jean-Pierre Néel](/w/index.php?title=Jean-Pierre_N%C3%A9el&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jean-Pierre Néel (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jean-Pierre_N%C3%A9el "fr:Jean-Pierre Néel")] (18 October 1832 - 18 February 1862), priest
